name: Data Interpretation — Reading Charts Fast
whatIsIt: Data Interpretation (DI) is answering questions from a chart or table — bar graphs, line graphs, pie charts and tables. You already know the maths (totals, differences, averages, percentages, ratios); DI is the skill of reading the RIGHT numbers off the picture quickly and then applying the right operation. In exams, one chart powers several questions, so accurate reading saves huge time.
whyNeeded: DI is a whole scoring section in Bank (IBPS/SBI), SSC, CAT and campus aptitude tests. It is also everyday literacy: reading sales dashboards, election results, cricket run charts and news infographics without being misled.
realLifeUses: Reading a shop's monthly sales chart
Comparing run rates or scores across matches
Understanding a budget pie chart or election bar graph
Spotting the trend in a company dashboard

vocabulary.meaning: One bar ÷ total × 100 — its slice of the whole.

story.title: Reading the Sweet-Shop Sales Board
story.text: The shop's board shows units sold: Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30. 'Which was the best month?' Prakash scans the bars: 'March — the tallest at 60.' 'Total for five months?' He adds: 20+40+60+50+30 = 200. 'Average per month?' 200 ÷ 5 = 40. 'What share was April?' 50 out of 200 = 25%. The owner smiles: 'You didn't recompute anything — you just read the right bars and did one operation each.'
story.extractedProblem: From the chart (Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30), find the total and the average per month.

visual.caption: Read the bar heights straight off: Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30. Total 200, average 40, and April is 50/200 = 25% of the total.

teachingGallery.title: Read the bar, then do ONE operation
teachingGallery.note: Total = add all. Average = total ÷ count. Share = bar ÷ total × 100.

examples.caption: Difference A − B = 10; total 50; A is 60%.

standardMethod.summary: Read the exact values off the chart, decide which operation the question needs, compute once.
steps: Read the required bar values directly from the chart (don't estimate if labels are given).
Pick the operation: total (add), average (total ÷ count), difference (subtract), share (part ÷ total × 100).
Sanity-check against the picture (the tallest bar should give the biggest number).

alternateMethods.name: Approximate-then-check
alternateMethods.whenToUse: For 'roughly how much / which is bigger' questions to save time.
steps: Compare bar heights by eye for quick 'bigger/smaller' answers.
Only compute exactly when the question needs a number.
alternateMethods.example: Mar is clearly the tallest, so it's the highest month without any addition.

workedExamples.problem: From Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30 — find the total units sold.
Using the same data, what percentage of the total was sold in April?

trickPractice.trick: Read the bar, then one operation
trickPractice.intro: Data: Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30. Answer each with a single step.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reading a bar's height wrongly (e.g. eyeballing 55 when the label clearly says 50).
commonMistakes.fix: When exact values or gridlines are given, READ them, don't estimate. Line the bar top up with the axis carefully before computing.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the wrong operation — e.g. adding when the question asks for the average, or forgetting to ÷ total for a percentage.
commonMistakes.fix: Match the word to the operation: 'total' → add, 'average' → ÷ count, 'how much more' → subtract, 'what percent / share' → part ÷ total × 100.

easy.q: Value of the February bar (Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30)?
Which month had the highest sales?

medium.q: How many more units in March than in May?

challenge.q: What percent of the total (200) was sold in February?
The question asks for the AVERAGE per month. You should…

options.label: Divide the total by the number of months

challenge.answer: Divide the total by the number of months

questions.q: Total of Jan, Feb and Mar?
To find a month's PERCENTAGE share you…

options.label: Divide that month by the total, then ×100

questions.answer: Divide that month by the total, then ×100

questions.q: Difference between the highest and lowest month?
Average of April and May (two months)?

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend to read a bar chart fast: use Jan 20, Feb 40, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 30 and show how to get the total (add), the average (÷5), and April's share (50/200 ×100). Stress: read the bar first, then do ONE operation.

revisionCard.summary: DI = read the right values off the chart, then apply one operation. Total = add all; average = total ÷ count; difference = subtract; share = part ÷ total × 100. Read exact labels (don't eyeball) and match the question word to the operation.

gameMission.title: Chart-Reader Challenge
gameMission.brief: Beat the clock reading the newsroom charts! Grab the right bar values, pick add / average / subtract / percent correctly, and file each answer before deadline.

realLifeProject: Find a real bar chart (news, cricket, a report). Read off three values and compute the total, the average, and one item's percentage share — exactly like an exam DI set.

rememberIt.hook: Read the bar, then ONE operation
rememberIt.unpack: Total = add. Average = total ÷ count. Share = part ÷ total × 100. Match the question word to the operation.

studentQuestions.q: Do I recompute for every question?
studentQuestions.a: No — read the values once. Most DI questions reuse the same chart; just pick the right operation each time.

studentQuestions.q: How do I avoid silly errors?
studentQuestions.a: Read exact labels rather than eyeballing, and check that the tallest bar gives the biggest number and shares add up to 100%.
